steps.prompt: Four friends on a Ferris wheel

graphic.name: ferris
graphic.caption: Four friends ride a Ferris wheel, each in a cabin on the rim. Join the cabins and you get a four-cornered shape with every corner on one circle.

steps.body:
A cyclic quadrilateral is a four-cornered shape with ALL four corners sitting on one circle — like four cabins on a Ferris wheel.
Last lesson you learned an inscribed angle is half its arc. That one fact hides a surprise about OPPOSITE corners (the ones across the shape from each other).
Here [∠A and ∠C](magenta) are one pair of opposite corners; [∠B and ∠D](orange) are the other.

steps.prompt: Drag D. Watch ∠A and ∠C trade off.
steps.subtitle: [∠A](magenta) and [∠C](magenta) are opposite corners. As one grows, the other shrinks — but keep an eye on their SUM.

steps.prompt: Make the four corners fit on the circle
steps.subtitle: One corner is [∠C](magenta) = 110°. Drag its opposite corner [∠A](magenta) until all four corners could sit on one circle, then check.

feedback.incorrect: Opposite corners of a cyclic quadrilateral add to 180°. If ∠C is 110°, then ∠A must be 180 − 110.

steps.revealText: ∠A = 180° − 110° = 70°. Opposite corners are supplementary — they add up to 180°.

steps.body:
In any cyclic quadrilateral, each pair of OPPOSITE corners is supplementary — they add to 180°.
[∠A + ∠C](magenta) = 180°, and [∠B + ∠D](orange) = 180°.
Here ∠A = 110° forces [∠C](magenta) = 70°. 'Supplementary' just means the two angles together make a straight line, 180°.

steps.subtitle: A quadrilateral is cyclic ONLY when all four corners lie on one circle. Sort each one.

items.label: All four corners lie on the circle
A square drawn inside its circle
A rectangle with all 4 corners on one circle
One corner sits inside the circle
One corner pokes outside the circle

steps.doneNote: A quadrilateral is cyclic only when EVERY corner lies on the circle — and then opposite angles add to 180°.

stages: [∠A](magenta) is an inscribed angle opening onto the arc from B through C to D, so that arc = 2 × ∠A.
[∠C](magenta) is an inscribed angle opening onto the other arc, from B through A to D, so that arc = 2 × ∠C.
Those two arcs together make the whole circle: 2 × ∠A + 2 × ∠C = 360°.
Divide both sides by 2: [∠A + ∠C](magenta) = 180°. Opposite corners are always supplementary.

steps.subtitle: A student finds a missing angle in a cyclic quadrilateral ABCD. One line is wrong — tap it.

lines.text: ABCD is cyclic, and angle A = 70 degrees.
Angle A and angle C are opposite corners.
Opposite angles add to 360, so angle C = 360 - 70 = 290 degrees.

steps.explanation: Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral add to 180°, not 360°. So [∠C](magenta) = 180° − 70° = 110°. (An angle of 290° couldn't even fit inside a quadrilateral.)
steps.hint: What do opposite corners add up to — a full turn (360°), or a straight line (180°)?

problems.prompt: ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral. ∠A = 100°. Find ∠C.

feedback.incorrect: Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral add to 180°.

problems.strongHint: ∠A and ∠C are opposite, so take the given ∠A away from 180°.

problems.prompt: ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral. ∠C = 115°. Find ∠A.

feedback.incorrect: ∠A and ∠C are opposite, so they add to 180°.

problems.strongHint: ∠A and ∠C are opposite, so take the given ∠C away from 180°.

problems.prompt: ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral. ∠A = 72°. Find ∠C.

feedback.incorrect: Opposite angles add to 180°, so ∠C is the supplement of ∠A.

problems.strongHint: ∠A and ∠C are opposite, so take the given ∠A away from 180°.

steps.subtitle: ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral and [∠C](magenta) = 117°. What is [∠A](magenta)?

feedback.incorrect: Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary (add to 180°).
